name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
إِذَا جَآءَكَ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقُونَ قَالُوا۟ نَشْهَدُ إِنَّكَ لَرَسُولُ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ إِنَّكَ لَرَسُولُهُۥ وَٱللَّهُ يَشْهَدُ إِنَّ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ لَكَـٰذِبُونَ (١)
```

```english
When the hypocrites come to you they say, ‘We bear witness that you are indeed the apostle of Allah.’ Allah knows that you are indeed His Apostle, and Allah bears witness that the hypocrites are indeed liars. (1)
```

```arabic
ٱتَّخَذُوٓا۟ أَيْمَـٰنَهُمْ جُنَّةً فَصَدُّوا۟ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ سَآءَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (٢)
```

```english
They make a shield of their oaths, and bar from the way of Allah. Evil indeed is what they used to do. (2)
```

```arabic
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ ءَامَنُوا۟ ثُمَّ كَفَرُوا۟ فَطُبِعَ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ فَهُمْ لَا يَفْقَهُونَ (٣)
```

```english
That is because they believed and then disbelieved, so their hearts were sealed. Hence, they do not understand. (3)
```

```arabic
۞ وَإِذَا رَأَيْتَهُمْ تُعْجِبُكَ أَجْسَامُهُمْ ۖ وَإِن يَقُولُوا۟ تَسْمَعْ لِقَوْلِهِمْ ۖ كَأَنَّهُمْ خُشُبٌ مُّسَنَّدَةٌ ۖ يَحْسَبُونَ كُلَّ صَيْحَةٍ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ هُمُ ٱلْعَدُوُّ فَٱحْذَرْهُمْ ۚ قَـٰتَلَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ ۖ أَنَّىٰ يُؤْفَكُونَ (٤)
```

```english
When you see them, their bodies impress you, and if they speak, you listen to their speech. Yet they are like dry logs set reclining [against a wall]. They suppose every cry is directed against them. They are the enemy, so beware of them. May Allah assail them, where do they stray?! (4)
```

```arabic
وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُمْ تَعَالَوْا۟ يَسْتَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ رَسُولُ ٱللَّهِ لَوَّوْا۟ رُءُوسَهُمْ وَرَأَيْتَهُمْ يَصُدُّونَ وَهُم مُّسْتَكْبِرُونَ (٥)
```

```english
When they are told, ‘Come, that Allah’s Apostle may plead for forgiveness for you,’ they twist their heads, and you see them turn away disdainfully. (5)
```

```arabic
سَوَآءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ أَسْتَغْفَرْتَ لَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ لَن يَغْفِرَ ٱللَّهُ لَهُمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلْفَـٰسِقِينَ (٦)
```

```english
It is the same for them whether you plead for forgiveness for them, or do not plead for forgiveness for them: Allah will never forgive them. Indeed Allah does not guide the transgressing lot. (6)
```

```arabic
هُمُ ٱلَّذِينَ يَقُولُونَ لَا تُنفِقُوا۟ عَلَىٰ مَنْ عِندَ رَسُولِ ٱللَّهِ حَتَّىٰ يَنفَضُّوا۟ ۗ وَلِلَّهِ خَزَآئِنُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ لَا يَفْقَهُونَ (٧)
```

```english
They are the ones who say, ‘Do not spend on those who are with the Apostle of Allah until they scatter off [from around him].’ Yet to Allah belong the treasuries of the heavens and the earth, but the hypocrites do not understand. (7)
```

```arabic
يَقُولُونَ لَئِن رَّجَعْنَآ إِلَى ٱلْمَدِينَةِ لَيُخْرِجَنَّ ٱلْأَعَزُّ مِنْهَا ٱلْأَذَلَّ ۚ وَلِلَّهِ ٱلْعِزَّةُ وَلِرَسُولِهِۦ وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٨)
```

```english
They say, ‘When we return to the city, the mighty will surely expel the weak from it.’ Yet all might belongs to Allah and His Apostle and the faithful, but the hypocrites do not know. (8)
```

## (Commentary)

The Surah describes the hypocrites (المنافقين), marking them with intense enmity. It commands the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to be wary of them and exhorts the believers to guard themselves against the characteristics of hypocrisy (النفاق) so they do not fall into its perdition nor be dragged by it toward the Fire. The Surah is Medinan.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «إِذا جاءَكَ الْمُنافِقُونَ قالُوا نَشْهَدُ إِنَّكَ لَرَسُولُ اللهِ وَاللهُ يَعْلَمُ إِنَّكَ لَرَسُولُهُ وَاللهُ يَشْهَدُ إِنَّ الْمُنافِقِينَ لَكاذِبُونَ» **When the hypocrites come to you they say, ‘We bear witness that you are indeed the apostle of Allah.’ Allah knows that you are indeed His Apostle, and Allah bears witness that the hypocrites are indeed liars.** (1)

The term "hypocrite" (المنافق) is the active participle of hypocrisy (النفاق), which in Quranic parlance refers to the outward display of faith (الإيمان) while concealing disbelief (الكفر).

**Lying** (الكذب) is the opposite of truthfulness (الصدق); it is the lack of correspondence between a report (الخبر) and external reality (الخارج). Thus, it is a description of the report, just as truthfulness is. Sometimes, the correspondence or lack thereof is considered in relation to the belief of the reporter (المخبر). In this case, the report's correspondence with the reporter's belief is considered truthfulness on his part, and its lack of correspondence is considered lying. Thus, it is said: "So-and-so is a liar" if his report does not match external reality, and "So-and-so is a liar" if he reports something that contradicts his belief. The first type is called objective truthfulness and lying (صدق وكذب خبريين), while the second is called subjective truthfulness and lying (صدق وكذب مخبريين).

His saying: «إِذا جاءَكَ الْمُنافِقُونَ قالُوا نَشْهَدُ إِنَّكَ لَرَسُولُ اللهِ» (When the hypocrites come to you they say, ‘We bear witness that you are indeed the apostle of Allah’) is a narration of their outward display of faith through their testimony to his messengership. Indeed, testifying to the messengership entails faith in what the Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) brought, and it includes faith in the oneness of Allah, the Exalted, and the Return (المعاد) — which constitutes complete faith.

His saying: «وَاللهُ يَعْلَمُ إِنَّكَ لَرَسُولُهُ» (Allah knows that you are indeed His Apostle) is a confirmation from Allah, the Exalted, of his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) messengership. He mentioned this — even though the revelation of the Quran and His addressing him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was sufficient to confirm his messengership — to serve as an explicit context (قرينة) that they are liars in terms of their lack of belief in what they say, even if their statement is true in itself. Thus, they are liars in their speech in a subjective sense (كذبا مخبريا), not an objective one (خبريا). Therefore, His saying: «وَاللهُ يَشْهَدُ إِنَّ الْمُنافِقِينَ لَكاذِبُونَ» (and Allah bears witness that the hypocrites are indeed liars) refers to subjective lying, not objective lying.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «اتَّخَذُوا أَيْمانَهُمْ جُنَّةً فَصَدُّوا عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ» **They make a shield of their oaths, and so they bar [people] from the way of Allah.** (2) etc.

Oaths (الأيمان) is the plural of *yamin* (يمين), meaning a sworn vow. A shield (الجنة) is a buckler (الترس), and it refers here metaphorically to that which one uses for protection. Barring (الصد) can mean "turning away" (الإعراض), in which case it refers to their own turning away from the way of Allah, which is the religion (الدين). It can also mean "diverting" (الصرف), in which case it refers to their diverting the common people from the religion while they are protected by their false oaths.

The meaning is: They took their false oaths, by which they swear, as a protection for themselves, and thus they turned away from the way of Allah and His religion, or they diverted the common people from the religion of Allah through whatever means of diversion they could, such as overturning matters and corrupting resolves.

His saying: «إِنَّهُمْ ساءَ ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ» (Evil indeed is what they used to do) is a condemnation of the deeds they persisted in from the time they became hypocrites until the revelation of this Surah.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ آمَنُوا ثُمَّ كَفَرُوا فَطُبِعَ عَلى قُلُوبِهِمْ فَهُمْ لا يَفْقَهُونَ» **That is because they believed and then disbelieved, so a seal was set on their hearts, and so they do not understand.** (3)

It appears that "that" (ذلك) refers to the evil of what they did, as has been said. It is also said that it refers to all that preceded: their lying, their taking cover behind false oaths, their barring from the way of Allah, and the vileness of their deeds.

What is meant by their "believing" — according to what has been said — is their outward faith with their tongues by testifying that there is no god but Allah and that Muhammad is His Messenger, followed by their "disbelieving" due to their inner selves being devoid of faith, as Allah, the Exalted, said regarding them: **And when they meet those who believe, they say, ‘We believe,’ and when they are alone with their devils, they say, ‘We are with you; we were only mocking.’** (Al-Baqarah: 14).

It is not improbable that some of them truly believed and then apostatized (ارتد), concealing their apostasy and joining the hypocrites, waiting for misfortunes to befall the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the believers, as appears from some verses of Surah al-Tawbah, such as His saying: **So He caused hypocrisy to take root in their hearts until the day they will meet Him, because they went back on what they had promised Allah.** (Al-Tawbah: 77). Allah, the Exalted, has described those whose hearts faith did not enter with phrases like: **and they disbelieved after their [outward] Islam.** (Al-Tawbah: 74).

Thus, it appears that what is meant by His saying: «آمَنُوا ثُمَّ كَفَرُوا» (they believed and then disbelieved) is their outward declaration of the two testimonies (الشهادتين), whether it was from the heart or merely verbal, followed by their disbelief through committing acts that entail disbelief, such as mocking the religion or rejecting some of its rulings.

His saying: «فَطُبِعَ عَلى قُلُوبِهِمْ فَهُمْ لا يَفْقَهُونَ» (so a seal was set on their hearts, and so they do not understand). Making the lack of understanding a consequence of the sealing (الطبع) of the hearts is evidence that sealing is a closing of the heart that results in its inability to accept the entry of the word of truth. Thus, such a person despairs of faith and is deprived of the truth.

Sealing the heart (الطبع على القلب) means making it such that it does not accept the truth nor follow it; consequently, it inevitably follows caprice (الهوى), as Allah, the Exalted, said: **they are the ones on whose hearts Allah has set a seal and who follow their own desires.** (Muhammad: 16). Thus, such a person does not understand, nor hear, nor know, as Allah, the Exalted, said: **and a seal was set on their hearts, so they do not understand.** (Al-Tawbah: 87), and He said: **and We set a seal on their hearts so they do not hear?** (Al-A'raf: 100), and He said: **and Allah has set a seal on their hearts, so they do not know.** (Al-Tawbah: 93). In any case, sealing from Allah, the Exalted, only occurs as a requital (مجازاة), because it is a form of misguidance (إضلال), and the misguidance attributed to Allah, the Exalted, is only misguidance by way of requital, not initial misguidance, as has been mentioned repeatedly.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَإِذا رَأَيْتَهُمْ تُعْجِبُكَ أَجْسامُهُمْ وَإِنْ يَقُولُوا تَسْمَعْ لِقَوْلِهِمْ» **When you see them, their bodies impress you, and if they speak, you listen to their speech.** (4) etc.

It appears that the address in "you see them" (رأيتهم) and "you listen" (تسمع) is a general address encompassing everyone who sees them and hears their speech, due to their fine appearance and eloquence of speech; it is not an address exclusive to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). The meaning is that they possess a handsome appearance and proportionate limbs such that if a viewer sees them, their bodies impress him; and they possess such eloquence and articulateness of speech that if a listener hears them, he is inclined to listen to their words because of their outward sweetness and fine composition.

His saying: «كَأَنَّهُمْ خُشُبٌ مُسَنَّدَةٌ» (as if they were propped-up [timbers of] wood) is a condemnation of their inner state. Wood (الخشب) is the plural of *khashabah* (خشبة), and propping up (التسنيد) is to set something up while leaning it against something else, like a wall or the like.

The sentence is intended to disparage them and completes the previous one. The meaning is that they have handsome, impressive bodies and magnificent, sweet speech, but they are like propped-up timbers—ghosts without souls, possessing no goodness or benefit, because they do not understand.

His saying: «يَحْسَبُونَ كُلَّ صَيْحَةٍ عَلَيْهِمْ» (They suppose every cry is against them) is another disparagement. That is, because they conceal disbelief and hide it from the believers, they live in fear, apprehension, and loneliness, fearing that their matter will be exposed and people will discover their inner state. They imagine that every cry they hear is directed at them and that they are its intended targets.

His saying: «هُمُ الْعَدُوُّ فَاحْذَرْهُمْ» (They are the enemy, so beware of them) means they are complete in their enmity, reaching its peak; for the most inimical of your enemies is the one who opposes you while you consider him a friend.

His saying: «قاتَلَهُمُ اللهُ أَنَّى يُؤْفَكُونَ» (May Allah ruin them! Where are they being led astray?) is an invocation against them for their destruction, which is the severest of worldly calamities. The use of the form *muqatalah* (fighting/ruining) instead of *qatl* (killing) is to indicate intensity.

It is said that it means expulsion and distancing from mercy. It is also said that it is a report (إخبار) rather than an invocation, meaning: the encompassing of the curse and expulsion upon them is established and fixed. It is also said that the word expresses wonder (التعجب), as one says: "May Allah ruin him, how poetic he is!" However, the apparent meaning from the context is the first view mentioned.

His saying: «أَنَّى يُؤْفَكُونَ» (Where are they being led astray?) is intended to express wonder, meaning: how are they turned away from the truth? It is also said to be a rebuke and a reprimand, not an inquiry.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَإِذا قِيلَ لَهُمْ تَعالَوْا يَسْتَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ رَسُولُ اللهِ لَوَّوْا رُؤُسَهُمْ» **When they are told, ‘Come, that the Apostle of Allah may plead for forgiveness for you,’ they twist their heads.** (5) etc.

Twisting (التلوية) is the intensive form of *lawa* (لوى), meaning to tilt or turn away.

The meaning is: When they are told, "Come, that the Apostle of Allah may plead for forgiveness for you" — which occurred when some of their betrayals and transgressions became apparent — they tilted their heads in avoidance and arrogance. A viewer would see them turning away from the speaker, being too arrogant to respond to his words.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «سَواءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ أَسْتَغْفَرْتَ لَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ لَنْ يَغْفِرَ اللهُ لَهُمْ» **It is the same for them whether you plead for forgiveness for them or do not plead for forgiveness for them: Allah will never forgive them.** (6) etc.

That is, seeking forgiveness and not doing so are equal in their regard. The equality of a thing and its absence is a metaphor for it not producing the desired benefit. Thus, the meaning is: your seeking forgiveness does not benefit them nor profit them.

His saying: «لَنْ يَغْفِرَ اللهُ لَهُمْ» (Allah will never forgive them) addresses a potential question: Why is seeking forgiveness for them equal to not doing so? The answer is: Allah will never forgive them.

His saying: «إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْفاسِقِينَ» (Indeed Allah does not guide the transgressing lot) is the justification for His saying: "Allah will never forgive them." The meaning is: Allah will never forgive them because His forgiveness for them would be a guidance for them toward felicity and Paradise, yet they are transgressors (فاسقون) who have exited the garb of servitude due to their concealment of disbelief and the sealing of their hearts; and Allah does not guide the transgressing people.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «هُمُ الَّذِينَ يَقُولُونَ لا تُنْفِقُوا عَلى مَنْ عِنْدَ رَسُولِ اللهِ حَتَّى يَنْفَضُّوا» **They are the ones who say, ‘Do not spend on those who are with the Apostle of Allah until they scatter.’** (7) etc.

Scattering (الانفضاض) means dispersing. The meaning is: The hypocrites are those who say, "Do not spend your wealth on the poor believers who have remained with the Messenger of Allah and gathered around him to support him, execute his command, and carry out his objectives, until they disperse from him so he can no longer exercise authority over us."

His saying: «وَلِلَّهِ خَزائِنُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ» (Yet to Allah belong the treasuries of the heavens and the earth) is a response to their saying: "Do not spend..." etc. That is, the religion is the religion of Allah, and He has no need for their spending. To Him belong the treasuries of the heavens and the earth; He spends from them and provides for whom He wills as He wills. Had He willed, He would have enriched the poor among the believers, but He, the Exalted, chooses what is most beneficial, so He tests them with poverty and commands them to be patient so that He may reward them with a noble reward and guide them to a straight path. The hypocrites are in ignorance of this.

This is the meaning of His saying: «وَلكِنَّ الْمُنافِقِينَ لا يَفْقَهُونَ» (but the hypocrites do not understand). That is, they do not understand the aspect of wisdom in that. It is also possible that the meaning is that the hypocrites do not understand that the treasuries of the world are in the hand of Allah and that He is the Provider, and there is no provider besides Him. Had He willed, He would have enriched them, but they imagine that wealth and poverty are in the hands of [material] causes; thus, they think that if they do not spend on those poor believers, they will find no provider to provide for them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «يَقُولُونَ لَئِنْ رَجَعْنا إِلَى الْمَدِينَةِ لَيُخْرِجَنَّ الْأَعَزُّ مِنْهَا الْأَذَلَّ وَلِلَّهِ الْعِزَّةُ وَلِرَسُولِهِ وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَلكِنَّ الْمُنافِقِينَ لا يَعْلَمُونَ» **They say, ‘If we return to Medina, the mightier will surely expel the abasest from it.’ Yet all might belongs to Allah and His Apostle and the faithful, but the hypocrites do not know.** (8)

The one who said this was Abdullah ibn Ubayy ibn Salul, as was the one who said the previous sentence: "Do not spend..." etc. It was expressed in the plural form to include his companions who agreed with his statement.

By "the mightier" (الأعز), he meant himself, and by "the abasest" (الأذل), he meant the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). He intended by this statement to threaten the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) with expulsion from Medina after returning to it. Allah refuted him and those who shared in his hypocrisy by His saying: «وَلِلَّهِ الْعِزَّةُ وَلِرَسُولِهِ وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَلكِنَّ الْمُنافِقِينَ لا يَعْلَمُونَ» (Yet all might belongs to Allah and His Apostle and the faithful, but the hypocrites do not know). Thus, He restricted might (العزة) to Himself, His Messenger, and the believers; therefore, nothing remains for others but abasement. He denied knowledge to the hypocrites; thus, nothing remains for them but abasement and ignorance.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Majma'*: The verses were revealed concerning Abdullah ibn Ubayy the hypocrite and his companions. This was because the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) received news that the Banu al-Mustaliq were gathering to fight him, and their leader was al-Harith ibn Abi Dirar, the father of Juwayriyah, the wife of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

When the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) heard of them, he went out to them until he met them at one of their watering places called al-Muraysi', in the direction of Qudayd toward the coast. The people advanced and fought; Allah defeated the Banu al-Mustaliq, some of them were killed, and the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) took their children, women, and wealth as spoils.

While the people were at that water, the people's water-carriers arrived. With Umar ibn al-Khattab was a hired hand of his from the Banu Ghifar called Jahjah ibn Sa'id, who was leading his horse. Jahjah and Sinan al-Juhani, from the Banu Awf ibn Khazraj, crowded at the water and fought. The Juhani cried out, "O assembly of the Ansar!" and the Ghifari cried out, "O assembly of the Muhajirun!" A man from the Muhajirun called Ju'al, who was poor, assisted the Ghifari. Abdullah ibn Ubayy said to Ju'al, "You are indeed insolent!" Ju'al replied, "And what prevents me from being so?" Ju'al's tongue became sharp against Abdullah. Abdullah said, "By Him by whom one swears, I shall certainly restrain you, and something else will concern you."

Ibn Ubayy became angry, and with him was a group of his people, including Zayd ibn Arqam, who was young in age. Ibn Ubayy said, "They have vied with us and outnumbered us in our own land. By Allah, the example of us and them is exactly as the sayer said: 'Fatten your dog and it will eat you.' By Allah, if we return to Medina, the mightier will surely expel the abasest from it" — meaning by "the mightier" himself and by "the abasest" the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). Then he turned to those of his people present and said, "This is what you have done to yourselves: you allowed them into your land and shared your wealth with them. By Allah, if you had withheld from Ju'al and his kind the surplus of your food, they would not have ridden upon your necks, and they would likely have moved from your land and joined their own tribes and allies."

Zayd ibn Arqam said, "By Allah, you are the abased, the few, the hated among your people, while Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is in might from the All-Beneficent and in affection from the Muslims. By Allah, I shall not love you after this speech of yours." Abdullah said, "Be quiet, I was only playing."

Zayd ibn Arqam went to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) after he had finished the campaign and informed him of the news. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) ordered the departure and sent for Abdullah. When he came, he said, "What is this that has reached me about you?" Abdullah said, "By Him who sent down the Book upon you, I never said any of that, and Zayd is a liar." Those of the Ansar present said, "O Messenger of Allah, he is our elder and chief; do not believe the words of a boy from the boys of the Ansar against him. Perhaps this boy was mistaken in his report."

The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) excused him, and blame toward Zayd spread among the Ansar.

When the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) set out and was traveling, Usayd ibn al-Hudayr met him and greeted him with the greeting of prophethood. Then he said, "O Messenger of Allah, you have set out at an unusual hour in which you do not normally travel." The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said, "Has the statement of your companion not reached you? He claimed that if he returns to Medina, the mightier will expel the abasest from it." Usayd said, "By Allah, O Messenger of Allah, you are the one who will expel him if you wish. He, by Allah, is the abased, and you are the mighty." Then he said, "O Messenger of Allah, be gentle with him, for by Allah, Allah brought you while his people were stringing beads to crown him, and he believes that you have snatched a kingdom from him."

News of what his father had done reached Abdullah, the son of Abdullah ibn Ubayy. He came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and said, "O Messenger of Allah, it has reached me that you intend to kill my father. If you must do so, then command me, and I will bring you his head. By Allah, the Khazraj know that there was no man among them more dutiful to his parents than I. I fear that you might command someone else to do it and he kills him, and my soul would not let me look at the killer of Abdullah ibn Ubayy walking among the people without me killing him, thus killing a believer for a disbeliever and entering the Fire." He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said, "Rather, be gentle with him and maintain good companionship with him as long as he remains with us."

They said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) traveled with the people that day until evening, and through their night until morning, and the early part of that day until the sun bothered them. Then he halted with the people, and as soon as they felt the touch of the ground, they fell asleep. He only did that to distract the people from the talk that had come from Abdullah ibn Ubayy.

Then he set out with the people until he halted at a water in the Hijaz, slightly above al-Baqi', called Buq'a. A strong wind blew that bothered them and they feared it. The she-camel of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) went astray during the night. He said, "Today, a hypocrite of great hypocrisy has died in Medina." It was asked, "Who is he?" He said, "Rifa'ah." A man from the hypocrites said, "How does he claim to know the Unseen (الغيب) yet he does not know the location of his she-camel? Does the one who brings him revelation not inform him?" Then Gabriel came to him and informed him of the hypocrite's statement and the location of the she-camel. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) informed his companions of this and said, "I do not claim that I know the Unseen, and I do not know it, but Allah, the Exalted, has informed me of the hypocrite's statement and the location of my she-camel. She is in the mountain pass." It was exactly as he said; they brought her, and that hypocrite believed.

When they arrived in Medina, they found Rifa'ah ibn Zayd in the coffin, one of the Banu Qaynuqa', and he was one of the great men of the Jews; he had died that day.

Zayd ibn Arqam said: When the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) reached Medina, I sat in the house because of the distress and shame I felt. Then Surah al-Munafiqun was revealed in confirmation of Zayd and in refutation of Abdullah ibn Ubayy. Then the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) took Zayd's ear, lifted him from the saddle, and said, "O boy, your mouth spoke the truth, your ears preserved it, and your heart understood it. Allah has sent down Quran regarding what you said."

Abdullah ibn Ubayy was near Medina, and when he wanted to enter it, his son Abdullah ibn Abdullah ibn Ubayy came and knelt his camel at the crossroads of Medina. He said, "What is the matter with you, woe to you?" He replied, "By Allah, you shall not enter it except by permission of the Messenger of Allah, and you shall know today who is the mightier and who is the abasest." Abdullah complained about his son to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), who sent word to him to let him enter. He said, "Since the command of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) has come, then yes." So he entered, and he did not remain but a few days until he fell ill and died.

When these verses were revealed and Abdullah's lie became clear, it was said to him, "Severe verses have been revealed concerning you, so go to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) that he may seek forgiveness for you." He twisted his head and said, "You commanded me to believe, and I believed; you commanded me to give the zakat of my wealth, and I gave it; nothing remains but that I prostrate to Muhammad." Then was revealed: «وَإِذا قِيلَ لَهُمْ تَعالَوْا يَسْتَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ رَسُولُ اللهِ لَوَّوْا رُؤُسَهُمْ» until His saying «لا يَعْلَمُونَ».

**I say:** What he mentioned of the story is taken from various narrations reported from Zayd ibn Arqam, Ibn Abbas, Ikrimah, Muhammad ibn Sirin, Ibn Ishaq, and others, where the accounts of some have been merged with others.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi: Regarding His saying, the Exalted: «إِذا جاءَكَ الْمُنافِقُونَ» the verse — he said: It was revealed during the campaign of al-Muraysi', which was the campaign of Banu al-Mustaliq in the fifth year of the Hijrah. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) had gone out for it, and when he returned, he halted at a well where the water was scarce.

Anas ibn Sayyar, an ally of the Ansar, and Jahjah ibn Sa'id al-Ghifari, a hired hand of Umar ibn al-Khattab, met at the well. Sayyar's bucket became entangled with Jahjah's bucket. Sayyar said, "My bucket!" and Jahjah said, "My bucket!" Jahjah struck Sayyar's face and blood flowed from it. Sayyar called out to the Khazraj and Jahjah called out to Quraysh. The people took up arms and sedition (الفتنة) was about to occur.

Abdullah ibn Ubayy heard the call and said, "What is this?" They informed him of the news, and he became extremely angry. Then he said, "I was averse to this journey. I am the mightiest of the Arabs; I did not think I would live to hear the like of this, and let there be no change from me."

Then he turned to his companions and said, "This is your doing: you gave them places in your homes, shared your wealth with them, protected them with your lives, and exposed your necks to killing so your women became widows and your children became orphans. If you had expelled them, they would have been dependents on others." Then he said, "If we return to Medina, the mightier will surely expel the abasest from it."

Among the people was Zayd ibn Arqam, who was a boy approaching puberty. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was in the shade of a tree at midday, and with him were a group of his companions from the Muhajirun and Ansar. Zayd came and informed him of what Abdullah ibn Ubayy had said. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said, "Perhaps you were mistaken, O boy." He said, "No, by Allah, I was not mistaken." He said, "Perhaps you were angry with him?" He said, "No, by Allah, I was not angry with him." He said, "Perhaps he spoke foolishly to you?" He said, "No, by Allah."

The Messenger of Allah said to Shuqran, his freedman, "Saddle up." So he saddled his mount and he rode. The people heard of this and said, "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) would not set out at such a time." The people set out, and Sa'd ibn Ubadah caught up with him and said, "Peace be upon you, O Messenger of Allah, and the mercy of Allah and His blessings." He said, "And upon you be peace." He said, "You would not normally set out at such a time." He said, "Have you not heard the statement of your companion?" He said, "And which companion of ours besides you, O Messenger of Allah?" He said, "Abdullah ibn Ubayy; he claimed that if he returns to Medina, the mightier will surely expel the abasest from it." He said, "O Messenger of Allah, you and your companions are the mightier, and he and his companions are the abased."

The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) traveled his entire day and no one spoke to him. The Khazraj turned to Abdullah ibn Ubayy, rebuking him. Abdullah swore that he had said nothing of the sort. They said, "Then come with us to the Messenger of Allah so we may apologize to him," but he twisted his neck away.

When night fell, the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) traveled his entire night and they did not halt except for prayer. On the next day, the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) halted and his companions halted, and the ground became a bed (أمهدهم) for them due to the exhaustion that had afflicted them. Abdullah ibn Ubayy came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and swore to him that he had not said that, and that he bears witness that there is no god but Allah and that you are the Messenger of Allah, and that Zayd had lied against me. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) accepted it from him, and the Khazraj turned to Zayd ibn Arqam, insulting him and saying to him, "You lied against Abdullah, our chief."

When the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) set out, Zayd was with him saying, "O Allah, You know that I did not lie against Abdullah ibn Ubayy." He had not traveled but a little when the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was overtaken by the state of semi-consciousness (البرحاء) that would take him during the descent of revelation. He became heavy until his she-camel almost knelt from the weight of the revelation. Then it left the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) while he was wiping the sweat from his forehead. Then he took Zayd ibn Arqam's ear, lifted him from the saddle, and said, "O boy, your statement was true and your heart preserved it, and Allah has sent down Quran regarding what you said."

When he halted, he gathered his companions and recited to them Surah al-Munafiqun: «بِسْمِ اللهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ إِذا جاءَكَ الْمُنافِقُونَ» until His saying «وَلكِنَّ الْمُنافِقِينَ لا يَعْلَمُونَ». Thus Allah exposed Abdullah ibn Ubayy.

Also in the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding His saying: «كَأَنَّهُمْ خُشُبٌ مُسَنَّدَةٌ» — He says: They do not hear nor understand. «يَحْسَبُونَ كُلَّ صَيْحَةٍ عَلَيْهِمْ» — meaning every sound. «هُمُ الْعَدُوُّ فَاحْذَرْهُمْ قاتَلَهُمُ اللهُ أَنَّى يُؤْفَكُونَ».

When Allah informed His Messenger of their news, their tribes went to them and said, "Woe to you, you have been exposed! Go to the Messenger of Allah that he may seek forgiveness for you." But they twisted their heads and were indifferent to seeking forgiveness. Allah says: «وَإِذا قِيلَ لَهُمْ تَعالَوْا يَسْتَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ رَسُولُ اللهِ لَوَّوْا رُؤُسَهُمْ وَرَأَيْتَهُمْ يَصُدُّونَ وَهُمْ مُسْتَكْبِرُونَ».

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain to Sama'ah from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "Indeed Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He, has delegated to the believer all his affairs, but He has not delegated to him that he should abase himself. Have you not seen the saying of Allah, the Glorified and Exalted, here: «لِلَّهِ الْعِزَّةُ وَلِرَسُولِهِ وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ» (Yet all might belongs to Allah and His Apostle and the faithful)? The believer ought to be mighty (عزيزا) and not be abased (ذليلا)."

**I say:** This meaning has been narrated with its chain from Dawud al-Raqqi and al-Hasan al-Ahmasi, and by another path from Sama'ah.

In it, with its chain from Mufaddal ibn Umar, who said: Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: "It is not fitting for a believer to abase himself." I asked, "How does he abase himself?" He said, "By entering into that for which he must apologize."

## (A Discussion on Hypocrisy in the Early Days of Islam)

The Quran pays immense attention to the matter of the hypocrites (المنافقين) and launches a violent assault upon them by mentioning their evil character, their lies, their deceptions, their intrigues, and the seditions (الفتن) they instigated against the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the Muslims. Mention of them is repeated in the Quranic Surahs such as al-Baqarah, Al 'Imran, al-Nisa', al-Ma'idah, al-Anfal, al-Tawbah, al-Ankabut, al-Ahzab, al-Fath, al-Hadid, al-Hashr, al-Munafiqun, and al-Tahrim.

Allah has threatened them in His speech with the severest of threats: in this world by sealing their hearts, placing a veil over their hearing and their sight, taking away their light, and leaving them in darknesses where they cannot see; and in the Hereafter by placing them in the lowest depth of the Fire.

This is only because of the severity of the calamities that befell Islam and the Muslims from their plotting, their cunning, and their various intrigues. Neither the polytheists, nor the Jews, nor the Christians attained from the religion of Allah what they attained. It is sufficient for you regarding them His saying, the Exalted, to His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) pointing to them: «هُمُ الْعَدُوُّ فَاحْذَرْهُمْ» **They are the enemy, so beware of them.** (Al-Munafiqun: 4).

The effects of their intrigues and plots appeared as soon as the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) migrated to Medina. Mention of them appeared in Surah al-Baqarah, which was revealed — according to what is said — at the head of six months after the Hijrah. Then in other Surahs revealed afterward, referring to matters of their intrigues and types of their plots, such as their withdrawal from the Islamic army on the day of Uhud — they were approximately one-third of it — their forming alliances with the Jews and inciting them against the Muslims, their building of the Mosque of Harm (مسجد الضرار), their spreading the "Affair of the Slander" (حديث الإفك), their instigating sedition in the story of the water-skin and the story of the mountain pass (العقبة), and other things referred to by the verses. Their matter in corruption and overturning things against the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) reached such a point that Allah threatened them with the like of His saying: **If the hypocrites and those in whose hearts is a sickness and the rumor-mongers in Medina do not desist, We will surely rouse you against them, then they will not be your neighbors in it except for a little while. Accursed, wherever they are found, they shall be seized and put to death.** (Al-Ahzab: 60-61).

The reports have become widespread and numerous that Abdullah ibn Ubayy ibn Salul and his companions were among the hypocrites, and they were the ones who used to overturn matters against the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and wait for misfortunes to befall him. They were known to the believers, and they constituted nearly one-third of the people. They were the ones who deserted the believers on the day of Uhud, separating from them and returning to Medina saying, "If we knew there would be fighting, we would have followed you." These were Abdullah ibn Ubayy and his companions.

From here, some have mentioned that the movement of hypocrisy (النفاق) began with the entry of Islam into Medina and continued until near the death of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

This is what a group of them mentioned, but reflection on the events of the Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) time, deep consideration of the seditions that occurred after his departure, and attention to the active nature of society (طبيعة الاجتماع) lead to a different view:

Firstly: There is no convincing evidence for the absence of hypocrisy seeping into the followers of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), the believers in Mecca before the Hijrah. The argument of the one who says: "The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the Muslims in Mecca before the Hijrah were not of such power, influence, and vast means that people would fear them and be wary of them, or hope for good from them such that they would display faith to them outwardly and seek proximity to them through Islam; rather, they were oppressed, tested, and tortured at the hands of the stalwarts of Quraysh and the polytheists of Mecca who were hostile to them and stubborn against the truth — unlike the state of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in Medina after the Hijrah, for he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) migrated to it having gained supporters from the Aws and Khazraj and having secured a pledge from their strong men to defend him as they defend themselves and their families; and Islam had entered the homes of their general populace, so he was supported by them against the small number who did not believe in him and remained in their polytheism, and it was not possible for them to announce their opposition and display their polytheism, so they avoided evil by displaying Islam, believing in him outwardly while they were in disbelief inwardly, thus they plotted and schemed what they schemed" — this argument is incomplete.

For power and formidable opposing force, and the hope for immediate good and quick gain, are not the exclusive causes of hypocrisy such that one could judge the absence of hypocrisy due to their absence. We often find in societies men who follow every caller and gather around every shouter, not caring about the opposition of the crushing, overwhelming opposing powers, living in danger and persisting in that in the hope that they might one day succeed in carrying out their aims, and rule over the people by independently managing the mill of society and attaining high status in the land. The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to mention in his call to his people that if they believed in him and followed him, they would be the kings of the earth.

Thus, it is intellectually possible that some of those who believed in him followed him in the outward form of his religion out of greed to reach their desire, which is advancement, leadership, and dominance. The effect resulting from this type of hypocrisy is not the overturning of matters and waiting for misfortunes to befall Islam and the Muslims, nor the corruption of the religious society; rather, it is its strengthening as much as possible and sacrificing for it with wealth and status so that matters may be organized thereby, and he may be prepared to benefit from it and draw gain for his personal interest. Yes, such a hypocrite plots with opposition and antagonism when something appears from the religion, for example, that contradicts his desire for advancement and authority, in order to return the matter to a path that leads to his corrupt purpose.

Also, it is possible that some Muslims might doubt their religion and thus apostatize and conceal their apostasy, as was previously referred to in His saying, the Exalted: «ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ آمَنُوا ثُمَّ كَفَرُوا» (That is because they believed and then disbelieved), and as appears from the tone of the like of His saying, the Exalted: **O you who have faith! Should any of you desert his religion, Allah will soon bring a people...** (Al-Ma'idah: 54).

Furthermore, those who believed from the polytheists of Mecca on the day of the Conquest (يوم الفتح) — most of them cannot be guaranteed to have believed with an internal faith of truth and sincerity. It is self-evident to anyone who reflects on the events of the years of the Call that the disbelievers of Mecca and its surroundings, especially the stalwarts of Quraysh, would not have believed in the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) were it not for the masses of troops that overwhelmed them and the flash of swords held over their heads on the day of the Conquest. How is it possible, given this, to judge that the light of faith occurred in their hearts in such circumstances, and that sincerity and certainty were in their souls, such that they all believed in Allah willingly and that hypocrisy did not creep into them at all?

Secondly: Because the claim that hypocrisy continued until near the departure of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and then ceased at that point is untenable. Yes, news of the hypocrites ceased with the departure and the establishment of the Caliphate, and their trace was erased, so there did not appear from them what used to appear of opposing effects and ominous plots and intrigues.

Was that because the hypocrites were guided to Islam and became sincere in faith, to the last man, upon the departure of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and their hearts were affected by his death in a way they were not affected by his life? Or was it that they made peace with the guardians of the Islamic government on the condition of ceasing interference, provided they were allowed what they desired, in a secret reconciliation after the departure or before it? Or was it that a coincidental reconciliation occurred there between them and the Muslims, so they all entered into the same watering place, and thus the clashing and collision ceased?

Perhaps sufficient reflection on the events of the end of the Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) era and the seditions that occurred after his departure will lead to obtaining a satisfying answer to these questions.

What we have provided in this section is a general indication of the path of research.
